core_takeaway: The first position did not beat the last position. Agent_1 successfully anchored Jewel as non-negotiable, but all agents had non-overlapping 100-value bundles, and the accepted allocation gave Agent_1, Agent_2, and Agent_3 full utility.
why_or_counterexample: The clearest counterexample is voting: Agent_1's own formal proposal would have left Agent_3 with nothing, but it received 0 accepts, while the later Agent_2 and Agent_3 proposals matching the shared Option A allocation were unanimously accepted. Agent_3, despite speaking last, ended with Quill and Pencil for 100 utility, equal to Agent_1's Jewel payoff.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Agent_1 moved first and framed Jewel as non-negotiable, but Agent_2 and Agent_3 immediately named their own value-maximizing bundles. Public discussion converged on Option A because the agents' top-value items did not conflict.
phase_notes.private_thinking: Available. Each agent privately identified the same Option A allocation as the stable unanimous path, with Agent_1 keeping Jewel, Agent_2 getting Apple and Stone, and Agent_3 getting Quill and Pencil.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: Agent_1 proposed an allocation that gave all non-Jewel items to Agent_2 and nothing to Agent_3, deviating from the public baseline. Agent_2 and Agent_3 both proposed the balanced Option A allocation.
phase_notes.private_voting: Available. Proposal #1 was unanimously rejected, while Proposals #2 and #3 were unanimously accepted. The accepted proposal was submitted by the last speaker and gave the last speaker full utility.
phase_notes.reflection: Not available; the transcript has no reflection entries.

mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1 used the opening move to make Jewel retention the default and later agents accepted that constraint, so there was some agenda-setting benefit from speaking first.

evidence.quote: I understand Jewel is your top priority

mechanisms.explanation: The tie was driven less by order and more by separable preferences: each agent had a distinct bundle worth 100, so the first and last positions could both be satisfied.

evidence.quote: I value Apple (47) and Stone (53) the most.
My top-valued items are Quill (45) and Pencil (55)

mechanisms.theme: Later proposals matched consensus better than the first proposal
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1's formal proposal did not become the outcome; Agent_2 and Agent_3 submitted the allocation that matched the earlier public consensus and preserved full utility for the last speaker.
The private votes rejected the first speaker's more exclusionary proposal and accepted the later equivalent Option A proposals unanimously, directly preventing a first-over-last advantage.

evidence.quote: also delivering 100 utility for Agent_3 in Round 1.
